cur-cancer-research-institute-cri-cri-im.why: This postdoctoral fellowship is precisely designed for dual training in immunology and computational science, making it an ideal match for your career stage and expertise. Your T-cell lymphoma research, single-cell RNA-seq mastery, and immunotherapy focus directly exemplify the computational immunologist the CRI program seeks to develop. With March and September deadlines and a three-year stipend structure tailored to postdocs, this program aligns perfectly with your current trajectory.

cur-damon-runyon-cancer-research-foundat.why: While T-cell lymphomas do occur in pediatric populations, your research portfolio emphasizes adult hematologic malignancy biology and computational methods rather than pediatric oncology specialization. A broader hematology-focused postdoctoral fellowship or one without the pediatric constraint would align more closely with your research identity and trajectory.

cur-national-cancer-institute-nci-nih-ca.why: The NCI ITCR Early-Stage mechanism (U01) was designed for emerging investigators like postdocs transitioning toward independence, and the program's explicit focus on novel machine-learning tools for cancer research matches your multi-omics integration and survival-modeling methods directly. Your single-cell and spatial omics expertise represents exactly the kind of informatics innovation ITCR prioritizes for sustained development.

cur-chan-zuckerberg-initiative-czi-singl.why: This RFA's emphasis on computational methods extracting biological insight from single-cell datasets is tailor-made for your scRNA-seq expertise and machine-learning integration with multimodal omics. Although the program's most recent cycle has closed, monitor CZI's Science funding page for successor opportunities, as the focus on AI-driven single-cell data analysis aligns with your methodological strengths.

cur-the-leukemia-lymphoma-society-lls-bl.why: LLS Scholar emphasizes patient-based clinical investigations and early-stage trials, which differs from computational-genomics work despite perfect blood-cancer alignment. Your risk-stratification insights could potentially frame as clinical research contributions, but program officers recommend reaching out directly to discuss how your computational work fits their clinical-investigator focus.

cur-nih-national-cancer-institute-nci-nh.why: The Parent R01 is the standard mechanism for hypothesis-driven cancer research and can accommodate your computational oncology and risk-stratification methods through the flexible modular budget structure. However, R01s typically require independent investigator status; you may be competitive if you can establish research independence and gather strong preliminary data from your postdoc work.

cur-nih-national-library-of-medicine-nlm.why: The NLM R01 explicitly funds novel AI/ML methods and computational biology tools that transform biomedical data, and your multi-omics integration and survival modeling directly address this mission. Your postdoc status presents a career-stage consideration for R01 eligibility; some NIH programs allow exceptional postdocs with strong preliminary data and methodological innovation to apply as PI.

cur-national-science-foundation-jointly-.why: This NSF/NIH collaborative program funds transformative AI and data-science methods in biomedicine, and your machine learning approaches to cancer genomics fit the high-risk, high-reward focus. However, the program is designed for independent researchers with established labs; postdocs typically need to partner with a faculty mentor as PI to be competitive.

cur-stand-up-to-cancer-administered-by-a.why: The Dream Team and Convergence grants have strong topical fit for cancer immunology and AI-driven multi-omics research, and your immunotherapy and risk-stratification expertise would be valued. However, these are multi-institution team initiatives led by established PIs; you could be a key team member if your mentor or collaborators are assembling a proposal, but you cannot serve as PI.

cur-american-cancer-society-leukemia-exp.why: Although LEAP targets blood cancer, its specific focus on RUNX1-FPD-driven hematologic malignancies (particularly AML) diverges from your T-cell lymphoma expertise. Your computational and genomics skills are valuable, but the disease-specific scope limits alignment, and the program typically funds more established investigators than postdocs.

cur-american-society-of-hematology-ash-a.why: The ASH Graduate Hematology Award is restricted to early-stage PhD students pursuing academic careers in hematology and does not apply to postdoctoral researchers. While you are no longer eligible, this award would be ideal for current graduate students in your lab pursuing hematology research.

cur-american-association-for-cancer-rese.why: Your computational risk stratification and high-risk immunotherapy research embody exactly what this program seeks in transformative cancer science, but the award is restricted to junior faculty, making you ineligible at the postdoctoral stage.

cur-cancer-research-institute-cri-cri-ll.why: Your T-cell lymphoma immunotherapy research and multi-omics approach align perfectly with CRI's focus on high-risk, high-reward cancer immunotherapy innovation. While this program targets mid-career scientists and you are currently postdoctoral, the $1.25M unrestricted budget and flexible structure make it worth monitoring as a target for 2–3 years from now when you have independent preliminary data and closer to research independence.

cur-american-society-of-hematology-ash-a.why: ASH's Scholar Award offers Fellow-level support that could fit your postdoctoral status, and your computational hematology research on blood cancers aligns well with the program's mission. However, this award traditionally targets MD hematologists or clinician-scientists, so verify whether PhD computational postdocs are competitive or whether your institution can nominate you as a fellow-level candidate.

cur-the-mark-foundation-for-cancer-resea.why: The Endeavor Award's multidisciplinary team-science approach and $3M budget align with your multi-omics integration and cancer immunology expertise, but it targets established collaborative teams, which is beyond your current postdoctoral status. Consider this as a methodological lead or co-investigator role on a faculty mentor's application in 3–5 years.

cur-the-v-foundation-for-cancer-research.why: The V Scholar Award's focus on launching independent research programs in blood cancer is excellent for your computational oncology and biostatistics strengths, but the program explicitly targets early-career faculty, not postdocs. Monitor this program and apply once you secure a faculty position—your innovative multi-omics approach will be highly competitive.

cur-pershing-square-sohn-cancer-research.why: The Pershing Square Sohn Prize celebrates bold, innovative rising-star faculty in cancer research, and your AI-driven multi-omics approach to T-cell lymphoma immunotherapy fits the innovation criterion perfectly. However, this prize explicitly targets early-career faculty, not postdocs, so prioritize postdoc-level fellowships now and revisit once you transition to a faculty position.

cur-u-s-department-of-defense-cdmrp-defe.why: Your T-cell lymphoma focus falls within the program's blood-cancer priority, but the Rare Cancers Research Program specifically targets rare malignancies. Whether peripheral or nodal T-cell lymphomas qualify as 'rare' under RCRP's definitions is worth clarifying with program officers, as this could significantly affect your competitiveness.

cur-paula-and-rodger-riney-foundation-pa.why: While the Riney Foundation supports blood cancer research and AI approaches, their focus is specifically on multiple myeloma rather than T-cell lymphoma, making this a poor disease-area match despite your strong hematology background. The rolling application model may be worth exploring only if you develop myeloma-adjacent computational methods in the future.

cur-aplastic-anemia-mds-international-fo.why: While AAMDSIF prioritizes new investigators in blood cancers and your computational skills are valuable, their focus on bone marrow failure diseases (aplastic anemia, MDS) does not overlap with your T-cell lymphoma research. Unless you develop shared computational tools broadly applicable to bone marrow disease, this is not a strong fit.

cur-american-cancer-society-with-fanconi.why: This RFA targets Fanconi anemia-related cancers, which falls outside your T-cell lymphoma and computational oncology focus. Although your genomics and AI expertise could theoretically apply, the disease-specific scope makes this a low-priority opportunity.

cur-kinship-foundation-searle-funds-sear.why: This award specifically targets researchers in their first year or two of an independent faculty appointment, which places it outside your current postdoctoral eligibility window. The flexible support for bold biomedical research aligns perfectly with your innovative computational oncology vision, so this becomes an excellent target once you transition to faculty.

cur-nih-nci-and-most-other-institutes-ce.why: The F31 predoctoral fellowship is designed for PhD students pursuing their dissertations, while your doctorate from 2019 places you well into postdoctoral training. This funding mechanism has passed for your career stage.

cur-multiple-myeloma-research-foundation.why: While your computational oncology methods and blood cancer focus are solid, the MMRF fellowship is specifically designed for multiple myeloma researchers, and your primary expertise is in T-cell lymphomas and immunotherapy in other hematologic malignancies. A lymphoma-specific or broader blood cancer fellowship would align more closely with your core research focus.

cur-lymphoma-research-foundation-lrf-lrf.why: Your postdoctoral position and direct focus on T-cell lymphoma position you as an excellent candidate for this award, which explicitly funds innovative lymphoma research at your career stage. Your multi-omics computational approach—integrating single-cell RNA-seq, spatial metabolomics, and machine-learning risk stratification—directly addresses the sophisticated biology the LRF seeks to advance.

cur-alex-s-lemonade-stand-foundation-als.why: The ALSF's computational data-science focus and open single-cell RNA-seq resources align well with your multi-omics expertise and machine-learning interests. However, their primary emphasis on pediatric cancers limits fit unless your T-cell lymphoma work specifically spans pediatric populations.

cur-international-myeloma-society-co-fun.why: Your blood-cancer domain and computational methods align with IMS's young-investigator portfolio, but myeloma is the foundation's core focus, not the T-cell lymphoma that anchors your program. The grant would be a better fit once or if you develop myeloma-specific research.

cur-conquer-cancer-the-asco-foundation-a.why: This award targets physician-scientists positioned at the threshold of their first independent faculty appointment, a career stage and likely training background that don't fit your current postdoctoral status. Revisit this opportunity once you establish your own research laboratory.

cur-the-hope-funds-for-cancer-research-h.why: Your postdoctoral position and focus on T-cell lymphomas—a lymphoma type explicitly supported by the Hope Funds—make you well-aligned with this award. Your paradigm-challenging approach to multi-omics integration and machine-learning-driven risk stratification exemplify the innovative research this foundation prioritizes.

cur-nih-common-fund-office-of-the-direct.why: While your multi-omics computational research is undoubtedly innovative, the DP2 is reserved for investigators holding or transitioning into independent faculty positions (assistant professor equivalent), and postdoctoral fellows are generally ineligible. You'll be well-positioned for this high-impact award once you establish your own research group.

cur-edward-p-evans-foundation-evansmds-e.why: EvansMDS enthusiastically supports AI and multi-omics approaches to blood disorders, making your computational methods highly attractive. However, the program focuses specifically on myelodysplastic syndromes rather than T-cell lymphoma, creating a disease-focus mismatch. This could work if your lab has active MDS research or if immune-driven marrow complications intersect your lymphoma work.

Like EvansMDS's other programs, this transition-to-independence award is locked to MDS research and pathogenesis, outside your lymphoma focus. The disease specificity creates a fundamental mismatch that your computational methods cannot overcome.

cur-gabrielle-s-angel-foundation-for-can.why: Gabrielle's Angel specifically targets early-career blood-cancer researchers with translational potential, and your computational risk stratification in T-cell lymphoma fits this mission perfectly. The $225k over three years supports exactly your career stage, and your multi-omics work demonstrates the clinical promise the foundation values.

cur-break-through-cancer-break-through-c.why: Break Through Cancer's AML TeamLab and Data Science Hub align strongly with your computational-oncology expertise and fund collaborative-team research rather than individual investigator grants. Rather than competing via open calls, explore this pathway through your [redacted] lab leadership or contact info@breakthroughcancer.org, as opportunities flow through institutional partnerships.

cur-rally-foundation-for-childhood-cance.why: Rally's Postdoctoral Research Grant tier perfectly matches your career stage and funding level for postdoc-scale science. However, Rally Foundation prioritizes pediatric cancer, and adult T-cell lymphoma falls outside their disease scope unless you're studying pediatric lymphoma subtypes or translating findings to childhood biology.

cur-leukemia-research-foundation-lrf-new.why: Your T-cell lymphoma expertise and computational methods align brilliantly with LRF's leukemia focus and their embrace of AI-driven research. However, LRF's New Investigator program typically targets early-career faculty launching independent labs, and postdocs are generally ineligible unless you hold a faculty appointment—revisit this when you transition.

cur-u-s-department-of-defense-cdmrp-cong.why: DoD BMFRP Idea Development Award welcomes innovative, high-risk computational approaches to blood disorders and supports exactly your methodological strengths. However, the program focuses on bone marrow failure diseases (aplastic anemia, MDS, inherited marrow disorders), not T-cell lymphoma—a disease-focus mismatch that would only work if your research addresses immune-mediated bone marrow failure.

cur-doris-duke-foundation-doris-duke-cha.why: Doris Duke's CSDA aligns perfectly with blood-cancer and computational research at exceptional funding levels (~$495k over three years). However, CSDA is designed for physician-scientist faculty (MD/DO or clinical appointments) transitioning to research independence, not PhD-based postdocs—explore this pathway once you transition to a faculty role.

cur-american-cancer-society-postdoctoral.why: This award is purpose-built for postdocs at your exact stage—it funds mentored training (three years, $217.5k total) to position you for independence while doing the blood cancer immunotherapy and computational genomics work you love. Your profile (blood cancer focus, AI/ML expertise, multi-omics integration) directly matches ACS's research priorities, and the December 1, 2026 deadline is immediately actionable.

cur-cancer-research-institute-cri-cri-te.why: Your computational systems biology toolkit—machine learning, multi-omics integration, risk stratification—is exactly what CRI funds to advance cancer immunotherapy, and blood cancer immunology is a core focus area. This $200k seed-stage grant is ideal for launching a competitive independent project from your postdoctoral work on T-cell lymphomas and spatial metabolomics.

cur-rita-allen-foundation-rita-allen-fou.why: Rita Allen recognizes exceptional early-career investigators pursuing pioneering research, and your computational oncology work on blood cancer and immunotherapy fits perfectly over the five-year award scope ($550k total). The main pathway is securing your institution's competitive nomination—[redacted] likely values your track record highly enough for a strong sponsorship.

cur-nih-common-fund-trans-nih-nci-partic.why: Your multi-omics expertise—spanning single-cell RNA-seq, ATAC-seq, spatial metabolomics, and multiplexed imaging—directly matches PRIMED-AI's mission to integrate imaging with genomic data for precision oncology in blood cancer. As a postdoc, you'd likely join as Co-I with your mentor as PI or as part of a consortium team, and the October 2026 deadlines are current.

cur-nih-awarded-to-institutions-nci-nhlb.why: T32 training grants are institutional awards, but if [redacted] or the Devavrat Shah lab holds a funded T32 in cancer genomics or computational oncology—very likely—you'd receive postdoctoral stipend support ($62–75k/year) plus structured mentoring. Check with your department's graduate-studies coordinator to confirm available T32s and your eligibility path.

cur-david-and-lucile-packard-foundation-.why: Your computational blood cancer research is a strong match for Packard's support of blue-sky early-career science—the $875k over five years and freedom to pursue high-risk directions would be transformative for your T-cell lymphoma genomics and immunotherapy work. One important note: verify that your permanent residency timeline (expected 2027) aligns with the application cycle, and be aware this is an extremely competitive fellowship.

cur-cancer-research-institute-cri-cri-cl.why: Your blood cancer immunology expertise and computational approach fit CLIP's clinic-lab integration mission well, especially for translating risk-stratification models to patient benefit. To maximize competitiveness, consider pairing your application with a clinical collaborator or translational partnership at [redacted] to strengthen the clinical translation component.

cur-conquer-cancer-the-asco-foundation-y.why: Conquer Cancer's disease-specific YIA competitions are natural landing spots for blood cancer researchers at your career stage, though unfortunately the 2026 CLL and mantle cell lymphoma competitions have already closed. I'd recommend monitoring their special-competitions page regularly—T-cell lymphoma or other hematologic malignancy categories may open in future cycles.

cur-nih-national-library-of-medicine-nlm.why: Your machine learning and multi-modal data-integration skills could eventually serve clinical informatics, but R01s are typically designed for independent researchers, and clinical informatics sits somewhat tangential to your core cancer genomics work. As a postdoc, you'd be better positioned as Co-I, leveraging your mentor's clinical data infrastructure and patient cohorts.

cur-lymphoma-research-foundation-lrf-lrf.why: Your T-cell lymphoma research and early-career postdoc status align perfectly with LRF's lymphoma-specific mission and structured mentoring model for emerging investigators. The combination of single-cell genomics, machine learning, and multi-omics integration directly addresses LRF's focus on advancing lymphoma biology and treatment, while the built-in mentorship will strengthen your transition to independence.

cur-national-cancer-institute-nci-nci-pa.why: The K99/R00 is purpose-built for your stage—a computational postdoc transitioning to faculty—and your blood-cancer genomics expertise and AI methods fit NCI's portfolio well. However, K99/R00 requires U.S. permanent residence at award time; your anticipated [redacted] (~March 2027) creates significant timing risk against typical NIH deadlines (Feb/Jun/Oct 2026), which may pass before your eligibility is confirmed.

cur-lymphoma-research-foundation-lrf-lrf.why: While your lymphoma research aligns with LRF's mission, the CDA is explicitly designed for physician-scientists (MD/DO clinicians) launching independent clinical research programs, not computational researchers. Your background positions you better for LRF's LSRMP, which welcomes non-clinical early-stage investigators.

cur-alex-s-lemonade-stand-foundation-als.why: Although your computational and genomic methods are precisely what cancer research needs, this award targets early-career investigators in pediatric oncology. Your research focuses on adult T-cell lymphoma and adult hematologic malignancies—a different disease population where ALSF's pediatric-specific mission provides limited support.

Like other ALSF programs, this mentored grant requires focus on childhood cancer; your expertise in adult blood-cancer genomics and immunotherapy does not align with ALSF's pediatric oncology mission, despite strong methodological fit in AI and multi-omics.

ALSF's innovation funding targets bold approaches to pediatric cancer; your groundbreaking work in multi-omics integration and machine learning for adult T-cell lymphoma falls outside their disease-specific scope, despite methodological excellence in computational oncology.

cur-st-baldrick-s-foundation-st-baldrick.why: While your career stage and blood-cancer expertise fit the profile, St. Baldrick's Scholar programs are dedicated to launching pediatric-cancer researchers, and your focus on adult lymphomas places you outside their core mission despite technical alignment.

St. Baldrick's funds hypothesis-driven pediatric-cancer research across lab and clinical domains; your adult hematologic malignancy focus and sophisticated genomic and ML methods don't align with their pediatric portfolio, despite strong methodological fit.

Although your multi-omics innovations and survival modeling are creative and collaborative, this award celebrates pediatric-cancer research specifically, and your T-cell lymphoma work targets adult populations beyond St. Baldrick's scope.

cur-alex-s-lemonade-stand-foundation-als.why: ALSF's translational pipeline bridges preclinical work into early-phase pediatric clinical trials—outside your current focus on adult lymphomas and blood cancers. While your work is translational in spirit (immunotherapy, risk stratification), the pediatric disease requirement is a fundamental mismatch.
This award is designed for undergraduate and graduate student researchers, not independent postdocs, and it focuses on childhood cancer—misaligned on both career stage and disease population.

cur-cancer-research-institute-cri-cri-ir.why: Your postdoctoral career stage and research on T-cell lymphoma immunology align perfectly with CRI's flagship fellowship for tumor immunology, and your single-cell RNA-seq and multi-omics expertise directly support the computational and immunological approaches the funder prioritizes. This is your strongest near-term opportunity to establish an independent research program in cancer immunology.

cur-nih-parent-announcement-national-lib.why: The K99/R00 is purpose-built for your postdoc-to-faculty transition, and NLM's participation for biomedical informatics makes your machine-learning and computational genomics approach highly competitive. Your work on survival modeling and multi-omics integration in blood cancer positions you well, though you'll want to confirm eligibility timing given your pending permanent residence (~[redacted]).

cur-niddk-division-of-kidney-urologic-an.why: The KUH division's focus on hematology and support for AI/computational approaches fit your blood-cancer expertise, and the training networks and K-award pathways offer flexibility for postdocs building independent programs. However, NIDDK emphasizes non-malignant hematology, so alignment is more general than disease-specific cancer grants.

cur-vera-and-joseph-dresner-foundation-m.why: The Early Career tier could fit a postdoc transitioning toward independence, and your computational and translational strengths would enhance basic-science proposals. Your primary focus on T-cell lymphoma rather than myelodysplastic syndromes creates a notable topical mismatch—you'd need to develop an MDS-specific research direction to be competitive.

cur-the-myelodysplastic-syndromes-founda.why: Your computational oncology and blood-cancer background would strengthen any MDS proposal, but applying would require shifting your focus away from T-cell lymphoma, which is your established research strength. Without an existing MDS research direction, this grant doesn't leverage your core expertise.

cur-american-cancer-society-research-sch.why: The RSG's scope—cancer research, genomics, AI—aligns beautifully with your program, but this award is reserved for early-career independent faculty. Once you secure a faculty position, this becomes a natural funding mechanism for your computational oncology research.

cur-the-taub-foundation-for-mds-research.why: The program's explicit focus on "independent early-career faculty" creates an immediate barrier, as you are currently a postdoc. Additionally, your research on T-cell lymphoma falls outside the MDS scope, making both career stage and topic misaligned.

cur-international-myeloma-society-ims-yo.why: This travel grant is tied to abstracts at the IMS Annual Meeting, which centers on myeloma; your T-cell lymphoma research would fall outside the conference's primary scope. The award is best suited for researchers already working in plasma-cell-disorder research.

cur-national-science-foundation-faculty-.why: NSF's CAREER award is exclusively for tenure-track faculty, and you are currently a postdoc. Your AI/computational approach will be well-positioned for NSF when you transition to a faculty role, particularly in cross-disciplinary data science.

cur-american-cancer-society-clinician-sc.why: The CSDG is designed for physician-scientists who maintain clinical practice; you are a computational researcher without clinical credentials. Your expertise and career path do not align with this program's intended audience.

cur-national-cancer-institute-nci-nci-ru.why: The F31 is exclusively for predoctoral students, and you earned your PhD in 2019, making you ineligible by career stage. This mechanism is not applicable to postdocs.

cur-nih-nci-nhlbi-nibib-nigms-and-others.why: The K25 is specifically designed to bring computational and machine-learning scientists into biomedical research, and you exemplify this profile perfectly with your expertise in multi-omics integration, survival modeling, and ML applied to cancer. Your work on T-cell lymphoma risk stratification and immunotherapy prediction is directly aligned with NCI priorities, making this an excellent fit for your postdoctoral stage—pending verification of work-visa authorization.

cur-nih-nci-and-most-institutes-centers-.why: The K01 is a natural fit for a PhD-trained computational scientist building toward independence with mentoring support, and your cancer genomics research is squarely within NCI scope. Your strong track record in single-cell and multi-omics analysis combined with machine learning will support a compelling preliminary-data case, though you'll want to verify permanent-residency or work-visa eligibility before investing effort.

cur-american-cancer-society-mission-boos.why: Your research on T-cell lymphoma risk stratification and computational prediction of immunotherapy response is translational and well-suited to ACS's two-stage mechanism for advancing discoveries toward clinical validation. As a postdoc with strong preliminary data in computational biomarkers, you could generate compelling Stage I data and transition to clinical collaboration and validation in Stage II.

cur-nih-nci-nci-nofo-par-25-135-nih-pare.why: The K99/R00 is purpose-built for postdocs transitioning to faculty independence in cancer research, and your computational-genomics expertise in T-cell lymphoma aligns perfectly with NCI priorities. However, you'll need permanent residency or an equivalent authorized work visa to qualify, and the award requires evidence of a definite faculty position or commitment within ~2 years—verify both requirements before applying.

cur-american-cancer-society-with-flatiro.why: You're an excellent fit for ACS's cancer focus and bring valuable machine-learning expertise that could enhance real-world clinical-evidence generation. However, the grant's primary emphasis on leveraging EHR/claims data (Flatiron Health) doesn't align naturally with your core expertise in genomics, multi-omics, and computational analysis—consider it only if your next project involves integrating genomics into large real-world clinical datasets.

cur-nih-nci-and-most-institutes-centers-.why: Your research perfectly aligns with NCI's cancer focus and your postdoctoral stage is ideal for the F32, but your pending permanent-resident status is a significant barrier—most NRSA awards require U.S. citizenship or authorized permanent residency. Plan to apply once you secure permanent residency (expected [redacted]), at which point this becomes a strong fit for mentored postdoctoral training.

cur-american-cancer-society-administered.why: IRGs are designed specifically for newly independent investigators establishing their own research programs, and you'll be an excellent candidate once you transition to a faculty position. In the meantime, focus on securing preliminary data and positioning yourself for K-awards and faculty recruitment.

cur-nih-common-fund-high-risk-high-rewar.why: The DP5 is expressly designed for exceptional PhD graduates launching an independent lab immediately after graduation—no postdoc—and you completed your PhD seven years ago, placing you well beyond the eligible window. You should instead pursue postdoc-to-faculty awards like K99/R00 or K25 that match your current career stage and timeline.

cur-nih-nci-and-most-institutes-centers-.why: The K08 is restricted to clinically trained investigators (MD, DO, DDS, DVM), and as a PhD-trained computational scientist, you don't meet the core eligibility requirement regardless of your research excellence.

cur-nih-participating-institutes-centers.why: The F30 is exclusively for MD/PhD and dual-degree students during their predoctoral training, and you hold a PhD only, making you ineligible for this award.
The F31 is exclusively for predoctoral students, and you completed your PhD in 2019 and are now a postdoctoral researcher, placing you well outside both the eligible career stage and population.

cur-nih-nci-historically-some-other-ics-.why: NCI discontinued K22 applications as of November 2025, rendering this program unavailable, though K99/R00 is the modern successor for postdoc-to-faculty transitions.

cur-the-mark-foundation-for-cancer-resea.why: The Mark Foundation prioritizes innovative, high-impact ideas outside conventional funding streams, and your computational risk stratification and immunotherapy prediction work in blood cancer exemplifies that vision. Postdoc eligibility as a PI should be verified, but the research and funding scope align exceptionally well.

cur-chan-zuckerberg-initiative-czi-accel.why: Your machine learning and multi-omics expertise could meaningfully benefit from GPU-scale compute resources, particularly if your work involves training or fine-tuning models on genomic and immunological data. The program's specific focus on foundation models requires careful alignment with your application scope.

cur-american-society-of-hematology-ash-a.why: Your blood cancer focus and computational expertise align perfectly with ASH's mission, but this award is restricted to early-career researchers outside the U.S. and Canada; your current position at a U.S. institution ([redacted]) likely disqualifies you despite your pending permanent residence status.

This program supports established researchers with unfunded R01s to bridge gaps in continuous funding, a context that does not apply to your postdoctoral stage and lack of prior independent research awards.

cur-american-association-for-cancer-rese.why: While career development is relevant to your stage, this award's specific focus on Tumor Treating Fields mechanism-of-action research does not align with your expertise in genomics, immunotherapy, and risk stratification in T-cell lymphoma.

cur-american-society-of-hematology-ash-a.why: Though this award supports blood cancer research, it is designed specifically for clinical fellows seeking protected research time; as a computational postdoctoral researcher without clinical fellowship credentials, you fall outside the intended career path.

This award targets mid-career and established investigators more than 13 years past the end of training, requiring substantially more career advancement than your current 7 years postdoctoral status permits.

cur-american-association-for-cancer-rese.why: This career-development award is exclusively for physician-scientists, and your computational oncology background does not include medical degree credentials.

cur-nih-nci-nhlbi-niddk-and-other-ics-ni.why: This exploratory grant is an ideal match for your career stage and research direction—it's designed precisely for the kind of high-risk, hypothesis-generating work in multi-omics integration and machine-learning approaches to T-cell lymphoma that postdocs with strong preliminary expertise can propose. The $275k budget over two years provides excellent support for method development and proof-of-concept work without requiring extensive preliminary data.

cur-nhlbi-division-of-blood-diseases-and.why: Your T-cell lymphoma and hematologic-malignancy focus maps directly onto NHLBI's blood-diseases research portfolio, and the program's multiple mechanisms (R21, R03, K awards) provide a strong range of entry points for postdoctoral researchers launching independent programs. This is an excellent strategic fit and offers flexibility in terms of grant mechanism and scope—definitely worth exploring for your next funding phase.

cur-nci-center-for-strategic-scientific-.why: If your multi-omics integration and machine-learning work includes developing, releasing, and sustaining software tools for the cancer-research community, ITCR's U01 and U24 mechanisms are an excellent fit for your computational expertise. However, if your work is primarily research-focused (discovery, application, analysis) rather than software engineering and tool development, this program may be less suitable.

cur-national-cancer-institute-nci-nci-me.why: While this honorific extension is attractive—offering up to seven years of flexible R01-level funding—you cannot apply for it directly; instead, it's automatically considered for high-scoring ESI R01 applications within the NCI payline. It's worth understanding this pathway for future planning, but your immediate focus should be on building and submitting a competitive R01.

cur-schmidt-sciences-schmidt-science-fel.why: This prestigious fellowship explicitly supports a deliberate pivot to a new, interdisciplinary research area at the postdoctoral stage, but your work remains in computational oncology and cancer genomics, the same research domain as your PhD training. Since you're not making a significant disciplinary shift, this program isn't the right fit, though it could become relevant if you decided to move into a substantially different field.

cur-doris-duke-foundation-doris-duke-phy.why: This fellowship is specifically designed for subspecialty clinical fellows in protected research years, and you're already positioned as an independent postdoctoral researcher in computational oncology. While your research focus aligns perfectly with the program's scope, the career-stage mismatch makes this opportunity not well-suited to your current trajectory.

cur-st-baldrick-s-foundation-st-baldrick.why: Although blood cancer and hematology are at the core of your research, St. Baldrick's specifically supports pediatric-oncology fellows, and your work on adult T-cell lymphomas falls outside their clinical-oncology fellowship pipeline. This would be an excellent program to revisit if you were mentoring or collaborating with pediatric-focused trainees.

cur-nih-nci-nhlbi-niddk-and-other-ics-me.why: The K08 Mentored Clinical Scientist award is restricted to physician-scientists (MD/DO holders) and requires clinical training in your research area—your postdoctoral path through computational biology and cancer genomics doesn't meet this requirement. If you were to pursue clinical training in the future, this mechanism would become relevant.

cur-national-cancer-institute-nci-nci-in.why: This is a predoctoral dual-degree fellowship for MD/PhD students, and you're already a postdoctoral researcher with a completed PhD, so you're ineligible. If you were mentoring or collaborating with MD/PhD students in cancer genomics, however, you might help them identify this opportunity.

cur-national-cancer-institute-nci-nci-pr.why: This award is specifically designed to bridge the final PhD years and early postdoc, and you've already moved well beyond that window—you received your PhD in 2019 and are now an established postdoctoral researcher. You won't meet the eligibility criteria for this career-transition mechanism.

cur-coefficient-giving-formerly-open-phi.why: This open RFP is exceptionally well-aligned with your work: you're at the precise intersection of transformative AI (machine learning, multi-omics integration) and cancer health, applying cutting-edge computational methods to improve outcomes in blood cancer. The rolling application process is also favorable for postdocs, and your track record in single-cell RNA-seq and survival modeling directly addresses how AI can advance cancer research and patient outcomes.

cur-the-v-foundation-for-cancer-research.why: This reinvestment grant is reserved exclusively for previously successful V Foundation awardees with strong track records, and as a postdoc you're unlikely to have prior V Foundation funding. This is not an appropriate target for your current career stage; revisit it after you've established an independent lab and secured an initial V Foundation grant.

cur-american-association-for-cancer-rese.why: Your T-cell lymphoma research, machine learning expertise, and focus on translational outcomes through immunotherapy and risk stratification fit AACR's mission excellently. However, this award explicitly supports 'physician-scientists transitioning from fellowship to faculty,' and you're currently in a postdoc role; you'd become a natural fit once you move into a faculty position, but you don't meet the career-stage requirement now.

cur-st-baldrick-s-foundation-american-as.why: Your expertise in single-cell RNA-seq, machine learning, and cancer genomics aligns with what this career-development grant supports, and blood cancer is central to both your research and the funder's mission. However, the explicit focus on pediatric cancer may not perfectly match your current work on T-cell lymphoma unless you're specifically studying pediatric cases, and the career-development framework typically expects faculty-track applicants rather than postdocs.

cur-conquer-cancer-the-asco-foundation-c.why: Your cancer research focus and computational translation of genomic insights into risk stratification strongly align with ASCO's support for independent cancer research programs. However, this award is designed for 'newly appointed faculty' establishing independent laboratories, and as a postdoc you don't yet hold that position—marking this as a high-priority target for when you transition to faculty.

cur-american-society-of-hematology-ash-i.why: This award's focus on blood cancer and computational oncology aligns beautifully with your research, and the annual research stipend of $30K would support your work on multi-omics integration in T-cell lymphoma. However, this is a faculty-development award specifically for newly appointed faculty from underrepresented backgrounds, and as a postdoc you're not yet at that career stage—you'd be an ideal candidate once you transition to an independent faculty position.

cur-stand-up-to-cancer-administered-by-t.why: Your high-risk, high-reward computational oncology work—particularly your integration of multiple omics modalities to understand T-cell lymphoma—exemplifies the bold, patient-impact-focused research SU2C champions. However, there is currently no open IRG application (as of mid-2026), and the program targets 'independent investigators,' which typically means established faculty running their own labs rather than postdocs—check back for future RFA cycles and revisit once you have faculty status.

cur-burroughs-wellcome-fund-career-award.why: Your track record in computational oncology and multi-omics integration reflects exactly the kind of innovative thinking BWF aims to nurture, and $700K over five years would substantially support your research program. However, CAMS is explicitly designed for 'physician-scientists' with clinical training—the award bridges the gap between postdoctoral/fellowship training and independence for those with MD or MD/PhD credentials—which differs from your computational-science background.

cur-doris-duke-foundation-doris-duke-cli.why: Doris Duke's emphasis on clinical/translational research and physician-scientist development touches areas you address through computational oncology and risk stratification work. However, this award is explicitly designed for physician-scientists (those with clinical training or MD backgrounds), and your profile is as a computational scientist without clinical credentials, which places you outside the intended applicant pool.

cur-national-heart-lung-and-blood-instit.why: Your expertise in blood cancer genomics and AI methods directly aligns with NHLBI's research priorities, particularly for long-term, innovative research programs. However, R35 awards are reserved for 'accomplished NHLBI-funded investigators' with an established portfolio of NHLBI grants and track record as an independent PI—you're not yet at that career stage as a postdoc, so this is a target for five–ten years out, after you've built an independent research program.

cur-google-google-research-google-phd-fe.why: This fellowship is exclusively for outstanding current PhD students in computer science and related fields, and since you completed your PhD in 2019, you are no longer eligible. While your AI and machine learning expertise would have been a strong match during your doctoral years, postdocs do not meet the eligibility criteria for this program.
